did_this_paper_use_ai_to_obtain_results_.details: The paper used a hybrid artificial intelligence framework for melanoma diagnosis using histopathological images. The method employed an Autoencoder for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction, followed by classification algorithms including Support Vector Machines, Random Forest, Multilayer Perceptron, and K-Nearest Neighbours to distinguish between melanoma and nevus.

is_this_paper_a_review_paper_or_a_resear.details: The paper presents original experimental work involving the development and evaluation of a hybrid artificial intelligence framework for melanoma diagnosis. It describes a specific methodology using an Autoencoder and classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, MLP, KNN) on a curated dataset of 313 histopathological images, reports new performance results (97.95% accuracy), and includes sections for Methods, Results, and Discussion typical of primary research.

title: A Hybrid Artificial Intelligence Framework for Melanoma Diagnosis Using Histopathological Images
brief_summary: This study developed a hybrid artificial intelligence framework for melanoma diagnosis using histopathological images to address the subjectivity and time-consuming nature of traditional analysis. The dataset comprised 313 curated histopathological images collected from 117 melanocytic lesions diagnosed at the 12 de Octubre University Hospital. The proposed two-step framework utilized an Autoencoder for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction, followed by classification algorithms including Support Vector Machines, Random Forest, Multilayer Perceptron, and K-Nearest Neighbours. Among the tested models, the combination of the Autoencoder with K-Nearest Neighbours achieved the best performance, reaching an average accuracy of approximately 97.95% on the test set. This result outperformed other models by 2 to 13 percentage points and demonstrated strong generalization capabilities compared to baseline comparisons.

lesion_types_studied.details: The study distinguishes melanoma from nevus. The dataset included 31 melanomas: 8 in situ, 12 superficial spreading, 1 acral lentiginous, 2 lentigo maligna, 7 metastases, and 1 satellitosis. One superficial spreading melanoma was cited as a True Positive example.

The study distinguishes nevus from melanoma. The dataset included 86 nevi: 30 intradermal, 12 compound, 5 lentiginous, 1 junctional, 6 congenital, 4 dysplastic, 4 Spitz, 9 Reed, and 15 blue nevi. Specific examples included dermal melanocytic nevus, Reed nevus, sclerosing Spitz nevus, dysplastic nevus, and deep cellular blue nevus.

all_task_types.details: The paper explicitly describes a binary classification task to distinguish between melanoma and nevus using histopathological images. The model outputs discrete class labels, evaluated via accuracy, sensitivity, specificity, F1-score, confusion matrices, and ROC/AUC curves. The primary model (Autoencoder + KNN) achieved 97.95% accuracy.

datasets_used.name: 12 de Octubre University Hospital Histopathological Dataset
datasets_used.description: A curated dataset of histopathological images collected from 117 melanocytic lesions diagnosed between 2022 and 2023 at the 12 de Octubre University Hospital (a tertiary referral centre). The raw dataset consisted of 506 (reported as 499 in Table 1) whole-slide images scanned at 200x magnification using an Aperio Versa scanning system. After quality control (removing defocused, partially scanned, or noisy images), 313 curated images were selected for the study.

use.training: Used to train the Autoencoder for dimensionality reduction and feature extraction, and subsequently to train the classifiers (SVM, Random Forest, MLP, KNN) on the extracted feature vectors. The dataset was split 80-20 into training and testing sets.
use.testing: Used to evaluate the performance of the hybrid models. The test set consisted of 63 images (49 nevi, 14 melanomas).
use.other: The dataset was augmented by rotating original images to increase the size by a factor of four (resulting in 776 nevus and 224 melanoma images for training, and 196 nevus and 56 melanoma images for testing) to mitigate class imbalance and enhance generalization.

statistical_methods_used.purpose: To quantify the uncertainty associated with diagnostic performance metrics (sensitivity and specificity) and enhance the reliability and generalisability of the results.
To analyze model performance by reporting False Positives (FPs) and False Negatives (FNs) for the best-performing models (KNN and RF).
To provide a threshold-independent evaluation of diagnostic ability, plotting sensitivity against the false positive rate to assess discriminative performance.
To evaluate performance on imbalanced datasets by plotting precision against sensitivity, providing insight into the model's ability to identify positive cases while minimizing FPs.
To measure and compare the classification performance of the hybrid models (Autoencoder + KNN/RF) against baselines and each other.

hypothesis_testing.interpretation: The paper did not report formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values or significance tests). It reported 95% Confidence Intervals (CIs) for sensitivity and specificity to quantify uncertainty and reliability, but did not perform statistical hypothesis tests to compare models or against baselines.

12 de Octubre University Hospital Histop.augmentation: Rotated original images by factor of four (Test set: 196 nevi, 56 melanomas)

Autoencoder + K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN).Confusion Matrix Details: 2 False Positives (FPs) (misclassified deep blue nevi), 0 False Negatives (FNs)

12 de Octubre University Hospital Histop.notes: The Autoencoder + KNN model achieved the best performance, outperforming other models by 2 to 13 percentage points. It demonstrated strong generalization capabilities. The two false positives for KNN were deep blue nevi, which share characteristics with melanoma. The RF model had a lower specificity CI (52-76%) compared to KNN. The study notes that the small test set size (14 melanomas) limits the robustness of sensitivity estimates.

architecture_used.main_model: Hybrid framework consisting of an Autoencoder for feature extraction followed by classical machine learning classifiers (K-Nearest Neighbours, Support Vector Machines, Random Forest, Multilayer Perceptron). The best-performing model was Autoencoder + K-Nearest Neighbours.
architecture_used.pretraining: The Autoencoder was trained from scratch using the study's dataset (no external pretraining mentioned). The paper notes that using pretrained CNNs was considered but deemed less suitable for this specific small dataset context.
architecture_used.input_size: 256 × 256 pixels (cropped from original Whole Slide Images). Original WSI dimensions ranged from 4583 × 6347 to 91,683 × 170,804 pixels.

architecture_details.encoder_structure: 7 convolutional blocks. Each block has a 3x3 convolutional filter with a pooling layer of size 2 and stride 1. Activation function: ReLU. Number of filters: starts at 8, doubling per block (8, 16, 32, 64, 128, 256, 512). Input dimensionality reduced from 256x256 to 2x2.
architecture_details.bottleneck: Convolutional layer with 512 filters. Feature map size remains constant (2x2).
architecture_details.decoder_structure: 7 deconvolution blocks. Each block consists of one upsampling layer followed by two convolution layers. Number of filters: starts at 512, halving per block (512, 256, 128, 64, 32, 16, 8).
architecture_details.feature_combination: Features from the bottleneck were aggregated using five methods: maximum, minimum, sum, average, and median values across all crops of a single image.

training_details.loss_function: Mean Squared Error (MSE) for Autoencoder reconstruction. Formula: min J_AE(theta) = min sum(xi - xi')^2.
training_details.validation_method: 5-fold cross-validation for classifiers to obtain mean and standard deviation of accuracy. No separate validation set used for non-neural classifiers during training.
training_details.data_augmentation: Rotation of original images by a factor of four.

preprocessing_techniques: Quality control and curation: Manual revision of raw Whole Slide Images (WSIs) to remove defocused images (64 images), partially scanned images with missing epithelial slices (47 images), and images containing text or physical labels introducing noise (67 images).

Cropping: Cropping large WSIs into 256 × 256 pixel colored slices to manage computational capacity and facilitate feature extraction. Crops with >60% white pixels (no information) were discarded, as were crops from the right and bottom borders that could not form complete 256 × 256 squares.

Normalization: Application of a Whole Slide Images (WSI) normalization algorithm involving conversion from RGB to Optical Density (OD) space, stain separation using an Adaptive Colour Deconvolution (ACD) model, calculation of a stain-weight matrix, and combination with a Stained Colour Augmentation (SCA) matrix to adjust colors and avoid perception differences.
Data Augmentation: Rotation of original images by a factor of four to increase dataset size and mitigate class imbalance.

data_augmentation_techniques.details: Original images were rotated by a factor of four to increase the dataset size and mitigate class imbalance. This resulted in the training set expanding to 776 nevus and 224 melanoma images, and the test set growing to 196 nevus and 56 melanoma images.

Part of the Whole Slide Images (WSI) normalization algorithm. A Stained Colour Augmentation (SCA) matrix was combined with components derived from an Adaptive Colour Deconvolution (ACD) model and a stain-weight matrix to adjust colors and avoid perception differences among images.

conclusions: The proposed hybrid artificial intelligence framework, combining an Autoencoder for feature extraction with classical machine learning classifiers, effectively distinguishes between melanoma and nevus in histopathological images.

The combination of the Autoencoder with K-Nearest Neighbours (KNN) achieved the best performance, reaching an average accuracy of approximately 97.95% on the test set with an inference time of 3.44 minutes per diagnosis.
The best-performing model outperformed baseline comparisons (including EfficientNetB3, DenseNet121, and ResNet50V2) by 2 to 13 percentage points, demonstrating strong generalization capabilities.

The KNN model demonstrated high reliability with a sensitivity of 100% (95% CI: 93–100%) and specificity of 95.55% (95% CI: 85–99%), indicating consistent detection of melanoma cases with very few false positives.

The Random Forest (RF) model also performed well with 97.95% accuracy but exhibited a lower specificity (97.77%) with a wider confidence interval (52–76%), highlighting a greater risk of false positives compared to KNN.
The study provides a subjective evaluation alongside objective metrics to better understand model hits and misses, offering insights into the model's behavior from a medical perspective.

The authors acknowledge limitations related to the small and imbalanced dataset size, noting that a larger and more balanced dataset is necessary to mitigate class imbalance issues and validate the robustness of the results.

Future work aims to enhance the diagnostic method by incorporating algorithms for counting mitoses and measuring distances to the epithelial zone, as well as exploring dual staining techniques and molecular analysis.

Future research will include interobserver concordance studies comparing the AI model's performance with pathologists to highlight how digital pathology can augment human examination with objective information.
important_remarks: The study is limited by a small and imbalanced dataset, with only 14 melanoma cases in the test set, which affects the robustness of sensitivity estimates and generalizability.
The model operates on cropped 256x256 pixel sections rather than full Whole Slide Images (WSI), potentially omitting contextual information.
The framework is binary (melanoma vs. nevus) and does not support broader differential diagnoses.
Performance has not been validated on external datasets or through prospective clinical studies.
Clinical translation faces regulatory hurdles, system integration challenges, and the need for clinician training to prevent over-reliance or misuse.
The two false positives in the best-performing model (Autoencoder + KNN) were deep blue nevi, which share histological characteristics with melanoma.
The Random Forest model exhibited a wide specificity confidence interval (52–76%), indicating higher variability in false positive rates compared to KNN.
No formal hypothesis testing (e.g., p-values) was performed; uncertainty was quantified using 95% Confidence Intervals.
The dataset was collected from a single tertiary referral center (12 de Octubre University Hospital), limiting demographic and geographic diversity.
The Autoencoder was trained from scratch without external pretraining, which may limit feature representation compared to models using large-scale pretraining.
